title: Exploring the Breakdown of Plastic Pollution

lesson.learn: Plastic pollution has become a global concern, with an estimated 8 million tons of plastic waste entering the world's oceans every year. This amount is equivalent to dumping the contents of one garbage truck into the ocean every minute. To understand the extent of this issue, consider the fact that the world's oceans cover over 70% of the Earth's surface, yet plastic debris has been found in every single one of them. The breakdown process involves both physical and chemical degradation, which is influenced by factors such as sunlight, temperature, and ocean currents.
lesson.simpler: Imagine a plastic water bottle breaking down into tiny pieces that are difficult to see. This process, known as degradation, occurs when plastics are exposed to environmental stressors like sunlight, wind, and water. As the plastics break down, they form smaller particles called microplastics, which can range from small fragments to fibers and beads. These microplastics can resemble tiny LEGO bricks, but their impact is far-reaching and detrimental.
lesson.keyTakeaway: A primary concern with microplastics is their ability to leach chemicals like bisphenol A (BPA) and phthalates into the surrounding environment. Research has shown that these chemicals can contaminate both aquatic life and human consumption. Furthermore, microplastics have been found in over 80% of tap water samples worldwide, highlighting the need for increased awareness and regulation around plastic pollution.
lesson.deeperDive: Scientific studies have demonstrated that plastic degradation is accelerated by various factors, including ultraviolet (UV) radiation, temperature fluctuations, and mechanical stress. For instance, a 2019 study published in the journal Environmental Science & Technology found that plastic bags can break down into microplastics within 12 hours when exposed to sunlight. Moreover, research suggests that microplastics can act as vectors for pollutants like pesticides and heavy metals, potentially harming humans who consume contaminated seafood. The effects of microplastic pollution are far-reaching, impacting marine ecosystems, human health, and the global economy.
lesson.funFact: In 1957, Swedish engineer Sten Gustaf Thulin invented the first plastic bag, made from a type of polyethylene called polyethene. Since then, more than 1 trillion plastic bags have been used worldwide annually, with the average person in Europe using over 500 plastic bags every year.

people: * Rachel Carson (Marine biologist and conservationist who sounded the alarm on plastic pollution)
* Sten Gustaf Thulin (Inventor of the first plastic bag)
* Dr. Jenna Jambeck (Environmental engineer and expert on marine debris)
* Ganga Ram Bahadur (Indian activist fighting against single-use plastics)
* Anjali Jain (Founder of the Trash Warriors movement)
places: * The Great Pacific Garbage Patch (A massive accumulation of ocean plastic in the Pacific Ocean)
* The Amazon River Basin (Home to some of the world's most pristine waterways and ecosystems)
* The United Nations Environment Programme (Global organization working on environmental issues, including plastic pollution)
* The Indian Ocean (One of the largest bodies of water on Earth, heavily impacted by microplastics)
years: * 1957 (Year Sten Gustaf Thulin invented the first plastic bag)
* 1972 (International Conference on the Human Environment, where concerns about plastic pollution were first raised)
* 2009 (UNEP launched its Clean Seas campaign to reduce marine debris)
* 2015 (Paris Agreement, which included commitments to address plastic pollution)
